Pages.Text: 27THE ARMY LISTINFANTRYHEADQUARTERS UNITSOFFICEROfficers in the US Army were individuals chosen for leadership positions, and given a presidential commission to give them the authority to carry out their duties. In infantry regiments, 2nd and 1st Lieutenants were platoon commanders, normally leading a platoon made up of three squads and their own HQ. Captains were in command of a company of three rifle platoons, a weapons platoon and other supporting elements. Majors could serve as company commanding officers, but would more typically be employed at battalion level. Officers were trained at the United States Military Academy West Point, the Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning, the college-based Reserve Officer Training Corps, or were commissioned from the enlisted ranks.HQ OPTIONSThese options are available to all units in the HQ Units section as long as all compulsory units in the same platoon are also equipped with the same option. For example, If your two compulsory units are Philippine Scouts upgraded to cavalry, any of your HQ Units may also be equipped with horses. • Any unit may be given motorcycles for +5pts per man• Any unit may be given horses for +2 pts per manCost- Platoon commander 21pts (Inexperienced), 30pts (Regular), 39pts (Veteran) - Company commander 42pts (Inexperienced), 60pts (Regular), 78pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 officerWeaponsRifleOptions- The officer may be accompanied by up to 5 men with rifles at a cost of +7pts (Inexperienced), +10pts (Regular), or +13pts (Veteran) each - Anybody may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each, or with a pistol for -1pt each- The squad may be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figure- One officer in the entire force may have Intelligence training for +50pts - By Air, Land, and Sea: Airborne, Rangers, Marines- If taken as Airborne may take up to 2 additional men for the listed cost.- If taken as Marines may take up to 1 additional man for the listed cost.Special Rules- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)- By Air, Land, and Sea (if taken)- Intelligence: An intelligence officer was typically employed at battalion level in US Army infantry units. However, each infantry regiment also had an intelligence major to assist the regimental commander, and an I&R Platoon, typically led by a lieutenant.Once per game, at the beginning of any turn (as long as the Intelligence officer is on the table), the Officer may act on intelligence gathered about the enemy force before the first order die is drawn from the dice bag. Roll a D6 - a Veteran officer receives a +1 modifier to this roll, while an Inexperienced one receives a -1 modifier. On a result of 4+, the player using this ability may choose an order die from the bag to be activated first. If both players are doing this in the same turn and both succeed in this roll, the results cancel out and the order die is randomly drawn, as normal.BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   27BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   2706/12/2024   12:20

BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST28MEDIC The field medic presents the wounded soldier with his best chance of surviving serious injury and can ensure that lightly wounded soldiers are returned to fighting fitness as rapidly as possible. Junior medical staff, such as stretcher-bearers, can accompany medics in the field. Medical officers were not always armed but in practice a pistol was often carried for self-defence.Cost23pts (Regular), 30pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 MedicWeaponsNoneOptions- The medic may be accompanied by up to 2 unarmed men at a cost of +8pts (Regular), +11pts (Veteran) each - By Air, Land, and Sea: Airborne, Rangers, Marines- If taken as Rangers may take up to 4 additional men for the listed cost.Special Rules- Medic (see Bolt Action rulebook page 113)- By Air, Land, and Sea (if taken) 29th Inf Div – probing forward in the Rhineland in February 1945 by Peter Dennis © Osprey Publishing Ltd. 29THE ARMY LISTCHAPLAIN Whilst clergymen have accompanied military forces for centuries, what is perhaps not as widely appreciated is just how far these heroic non-combatants were prepared to go to support soldiers of both sides, and the risks they took. On 20th December 1944, US Army Chaplain Francis Sampson left the heavily besieged Bastogne in his jeep to travel to an American field hospital he heard had been overrun by the advancing Germans. After being directed to a nearby village by a Belgian civilian, Sampson ordered his driver into the village even though it was clear that the settlement was in German hands. Sampson deliberately got himself captured and risked death so as to deliver medical aid to wounded soldiers. It was the second time he had been captured; he earned a Distinguished Service Cross after jumping into Normandy with the 501st Parachute Infantry Regiment where he delivered medical aid to both American and German soldiers.Chaplains of many faiths, denominations and nationalities have been decorated for bravery across a myriad of duties, and many have paid the ultimate price. A chaplain’s duties include holding religious services (sometimes under enemy fire), administering care and prayers to the dying (such as last rites) and acting as a direct link between front line soldiers and higher-ranking officers, speaking on behalf of the men to fight their corner for issues such as welfare, food and conditions in the field. Chaplains often accompany front line units in the very thick of the action and many have been decorated for facing enemy fire to carry wounded soldiers back to friendly lines for medical attention. The mere presence of an unarmed priest braving enemy fire is often enough to galvanize combat troops into action, because if the padre can do it, they should be able to! Be it for spiritual care, the extra influence to improve physical well-being, the knowledge of having another man willing to recover them if they are wounded or the comfort of the last prayers in those last seconds, a chaplain is a welcome addition to any fighting force.Cost17pts (Inexperienced), 25pts (Regular), 33pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 ChaplainWeaponsNoneOptions- By Air, Land, and Sea: Airborne, Rangers, MarinesSpecial Rules- HQ Choice: A force can include a single chaplain, who is not part of any of the platoon selectors, in the same manner as a company commander. - By Air, Land, and Sea (if taken)- Self-defence: see the Medic entry in the Bolt Action rulebook, page 113.- Inspiring Presence: Upon receiving his order die (except for Down), a chaplain may select 1 friendly unit within 6” and roll a D6, applying the following modifiers: Inexperienced -1, Veteran +1. On a 4+, the chaplain may remove 1 pin from the selected unit.FORWARD OBSERVER Forward observers are liaison officers responsible for coordinating the attack of aircraft strikes or heavy artillery batteries firing from far behind the battle lines. They are likely to be accompanied by a radio operator and other immediate attendants. We rate these officers as Regular or Veteran – those of lesser ability being unlikely to find themselves in such an important position.Cost- Artillery or Air Force Forward Observer 75pts (Regular), 90pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 Forward ObserverWeaponsRifleOptions- The observer may be accompanied by up to 2 men with rifles at a cost of +11pts (Regular), or +14pts (Veteran) each- Anybody may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each, or with a pistol for -1pt each- By Air, Land, and Sea: Airborne, Rangers, MarinesSpecial Rules- Infiltrators - By Air, Land, and Sea (if taken) 29th Inf Div – probing forward in the Rhineland in February 1945 by Peter Dennis © Osprey Publishing Ltd. BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST32INFANTRY SQUADS & TEAMSUS ARMY RIFLE SQUADA US Army Rifle Platoon typically consisted of three squads. Each squad consisted of twelve soldiers; one was armed with a BAR and the squad’s NCO typically was armed with a Thompson submachine gun, or an M3 ‘Grease Gun’ later on in the war. The Rifle Squad formed the backbone of US Army infantry regiments. Cost42pts (Inexperienced), 60pts (Regular), 78pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 NCO and 5 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles at +7pts each (Inexperienced), +10pts each (Regular), or +13pts each (Veteran)- The NCO may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts - The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)M1 GARANDDesigned by John Garand at the US Springfield Arsenal, the M1 Rifle (far better known as the M1 Garand) entered service in 1936 as the first self-loading (semi-automatic) rifle issued en masse to American troops, replacing the bolt-action M1903 Springfield rifle. Over five million would be made, with the US being the only major belligerent of the Second World War to issue a semi-automatic infantry rifle as standard. This provided US infantrymen with a significant advantage in firepower over their opponents, and the M1 was a popular, effective, and reliable weapon. General Patton would famously describe it as “the greatest battle implement ever devised”, and the Garand, alongside the Browning Automatic Rifle and M2 heavy machine gun, would become one of the most iconic American weapons of the Second World War.US Army Infantry BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   32BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   3206/12/2024   12:20

33THE ARMY LISTARMOURED INFANTRY RIFLE SQUADWhilst the US Army was better equipped than many others in terms of motorised transport for its infantry, only a certain proportion of regiments were fortunate enough to be equipped with armoured transport. Armoured Infantry Regiments were equipped with half-tracks and formed part of the US Army’s Armoured Divisions, mobile enough to keep pace with the tanks they supported. Given the more specialised role they were employed in, these regiments were equipped with a higher number of Bazookas rather than the light machine guns of their infantry division contemporaries, although some squads unofficially “acquired” BARs. Cost48pts (Inexperienced), 66pts (Regular), 84pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 5 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles at +8pts each (Inexperienced), +11pts each (Regular), or +14pts each (Veteran)- The NCO may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts - The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Selection: For each of these squads included in the platoon, you are allowed to include one additional Bazooka team in the same platoon (only in platoons that allow Bazooka teams to begin with).- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)- Mechanised Infantry: This unit can re-roll any failed order test that is required to mount or dismount a transport vehicle. In addition, if their transport vehicle is destroyed, this unit suffers D3 hits rather than D6 hits.ARMOURED INFANTRY MORTAR SQUADEach platoon in an Armoured Infantry Company included a mortar squad to support its two rifle squads. Normally led by a Staff Sergeant supported by seven enlisted men, the squad operated a single 60mm mortar, but was also able to field an M9 Bazooka that formed such a large part of the punch of the Armoured Infantry. Cost45pts (Inexperienced), 64pts (Regular), 83pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 3 menWeaponsRifles, one man has 1 light mortar in addition to his rifle (another man is the loader)Options- Add up to 3 men with rifles at +8pts each (Inexperienced), +11pts each (Regular), or +14pts each (Veteran)- The NCO may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts- The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Selection: A maximum of 1 Armoured Infantry Mortar Squad may be taken for each platoon. If this squad is included in the platoon, you are allowed to include one additional Bazooka team in the same platoon (only in platoons that allow Bazooka teams to begin with).- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)- Mechanised Infantry: This unit can re-roll any failed order test that is required to mount or dismount a transport vehicle. In addition, if their transport vehicle is destroyed, this unit suffers D3 hits rather than D6 hits.M-SERIES EVERYTHING! A brief glance at the vehicle section of this rulebook, or indeed at any list of American equipment, will very quickly reveal a naming convention that is simultaneously incredibly simple and logical, and prone to causing confusion in the uninitiated – the infamous ‘M-numbers’! Very simply, each new piece of a particular type of equipment would receive a sequential number, starting with M1, and then the next of that type would be M2, and so on. While a fundamentally sound system, the rapid introduction of many new weapons, vehicles, and other equipment would lead to some rather confusing possibilities: spare a thought for the GI sat in the back of an M3 half-track, cleaning his M3 submachine gun or sharpening his M3 bayonet, while in convoy with an M3 light tank and M3 scout car and passing an emplaced M3 anti-tank gun, while musing on the relative merits of the M1 rifle versus the M1 carbine and M1 submachine gun, all the while wearing his M1 helmet! BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST34ARMOURED INFANTRY MACHINE GUN SQUADEach platoon in an Armoured Infantry Company also included a light machine gun squad to support its rifle squads. As with the mortar squad, it was normally led by a Staff Sergeant but supported by eleven enlisted men. The squad’s M3A1 half-track was fitted with additional .30 calibre machine guns on the sides which could be detached and operated by its two gunners and they could field an M9 Bazooka for tackling enemy armour. 35THE ARMY LISTAIRBORNE ENGINEER SQUADThe US 82nd and 101st Airborne Divisions had engineer battalions to give them the support they needed on operations. The Airborne Engineer Battalion was a mixed glider/parachute formation with two companies landing in gliders with the heavier engineering equipment, and one company deployed by parachute with the initial waves. Airborne engineers performed the usual engineering tasks, with an emphasis on demolition work, which many of the Airborne’s objectives required.Cost75pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 4 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 5 men with rifles at +15pts each- The NCO and up to 2 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 1 man replace their rifle with a light machine gun for +15pts – another man becomes the loader- Up to 1 man can replace their rifle with a flamethrower for +30pts – another man becomes the assistant- Up to 1 man can take a demolition charge in addition to other weapons for +20pts- The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Engineers- Stubborn- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken) US Rangers advance through a French villageBOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   35BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   3506/12/2024   12:20

BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST36US PARATROOPER SQUAD The concept of air dropping troops directly into action was still new and unproven by the outbreak of World War II, and the US Army was quick to keep pace with other nations upon entering the conflict. The 82nd Airborne Division became the first airborne division of the US Army in August 1942 and deployed to Sicily in July 1943. By the time the 82nd and 101st Airborne Divisions parachuted into action in Normandy in June 1944, their ranks were made up of highly trained and motivated volunteers at the lowest levels; and at the core, combat hardened veterans of the Mediterranean theatre.Cost66pts (Regular), 84pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 5 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles at +11pts each (Regular), or +14pts each (Veteran)- The NCO and up to 2 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts - Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with a light machine gun for +15pts – another man becomes the loader- The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Stubborn- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)PATHFINDERS SQUAD The coordination of airborne assaults was, and remains, a highly complex task. The logistical challenge presented by placing hundreds, if not thousands, of men and their equipment miles behind enemy lines was immense, and with so much riding on success or failure it was unacceptable to have drops land miles from a designated drop point. This was where the Pathfinders came in. Pathfinders jumped ahead of the main force to quickly and accurately locate intended drop zones and then highlight these to the main force, either visually or via radio navigation aids.Cost90pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 5 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles for +15pts each- The NCO and up to 5 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts eachSpecial Rules- Stubborn- LZ Signals: If at least one unit of Pathfinders is not in reserve (i.e. is deployed or in first wave), then any friendly paratroopers, pathfinders and glider units in reserve may re-roll failed order tests to come onto the table from reserve.GLIDER SQUAD Glider-borne troops had training that was similar to that of paratroopers and their deployment was certainly no less dangerous. There was an amount of understandable rivalry between the two that, to some extent, continues to this day.Cost60pts (Regular), 78pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 5 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles for +10pts each (Regular), or +13pts each (Veteran)- The NCO and up to 2 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts - The squad may be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figure- If taken as Veterans, the squad may be given the Stubborn rule for +1pt per figureSpecial Rules- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)- Stubborn (if option is taken)BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   36BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   3606/12/2024   12:20

37THE ARMY LISTGLIDER MACHINE GUN SQUAD The flexibility of using gliders to move soldiers and equipment into battle allowed the Glider Infantry Regiments to organise themselves differently than their brethren in the Parachute Infantry Regiments. Each glider company had a Weapon Platoon to support its four rifle platoons, comprising of a machine gun section and a mortar section. The machine gun section was made up of two squads, each of up to six men and a .30 calibre machine gun.Cost55pts (Regular), 67pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 3 menWeaponsRifles, one man has a light machine gun instead of a rifle, another is the loader.Options- Add up to 2 men with rifles at +10pts each (Regular), or +13pts each (Veteran)- The NCO may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts- The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figure- If taken as Veterans, the squad may be given the Stubborn rule for +1pt per figureSpecial Rules- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)- Stubborn (if option is taken)RANGER SQUADThe organization of Ranger units evolved throughout the Second World War, from the highest levels down to the basic tactical element of the section. The TOE for a Ranger Assault Section from June–November 1942 put the 10-man section under the command of a sergeant, assisted by a corporal armed with a BAR. The remainder of the section consisted of two scouts, an assistant to the BAR gunner, and five riflemen. In February 1944 the Ranger Assault Section was reorganized slightly, with a .30 calibre M1919A6 light machine gun replacing the BAR (though a BAR was often retained or ‘acquired’ for more firepower). Sections were divided into two squads – a 5-man rifle squad and a 6-man Machine gun squad.Cost70pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 4 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles at +14pts each- The NCO and up to 2 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts - In a Late War force, up to 1 man may replace their rifle with a light machine gun for +15pts – another man becomes the loader- The squad may be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken)- Rangers Lead the Way! BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST38I&R SQUAD Each infantry regiment within the US Army had an Intelligence and Reconnaissance (I&R) Platoon whose job was to act as the eyes and ears and report directly to Regimental HQ, rather than being attached to a specific battalion. The I&R Platoon HQ would act as regimental intelligence staff whilst also leading the platoon’s two squads in the field, covering a range of duties such as patrols, observation of enemy activities or scouting terrain.They were primarily a passive unit, in theory at least, and were not expecting to engage the enemy for information. However, experience soon showed that the I&R men would often be involved in firefights and consequently squads began to acquire BARs to supplement their offensive armament.Cost28pts (Inexperienced), 40pts (Regular), 52pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 NCO and 3 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 5 men with rifles at +7pts each (Inexperienced), 10pts each (Regular), or 13pts each (Veteran) - The NCO and up to 2 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts Special Rules- Recon Troops: The unit will spot hidden enemy units at 18” rather than the normal 12”.- Regimental Units: A force can include a maximum of 2 I&R Squads.  US Marine machine gun teamBOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   38BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   3806/12/2024   12:21

39THE ARMY LISTUS MARINE SQUAD Before the war, the Marine Corps was used as a naval intervention force. For this reason, and in view of the tensions with Japan, many units were already on Midway, in the Philippines and on Guam when the war began. The first amphibious operation undertaken by the Marines was at Guadalcanal. The Japanese didn’t oppose the landing but retreated to defend the island’s interior. At Tarawa, a tenacious defence of the beaches caused many casualties and led to significant changes in the Marines’ equipment.Cost49pts (Inexperienced), 70pts (Regular), 91pts (Veteran)PeriodE/M/LComposition1 NCO and 6 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles for +7pts each (Inexperienced), +10pts each (Regular),or +13pts each (Veteran)- The NCO and up to 2 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 1 man may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts. BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST40USMC RAIDERS SQUAD By order of the President, two USMC Raider battalions were formed in 1942 to undertake commando-style attacks on lightly defended Japanese islands (the name Marine Commandos was considered and rejected). The men of these battalions were handpicked, well-trained and highly motivated. One battalion was trained by Lieutenant Colonel Evans J. Carlson and the other by Lieutenant Colonel Merritt A. “Red Mike” Edson. While Edson took a conventional approach, Carlson had fought alongside the Communist 8th Route Army in China. He introduced many of the guerrilla tactics and organisation he’d learned to his battalion, such as using ten-man squads and three-man fire teams, refinements that would eventually be adopted by the whole USMC. Carlson is also credited with introducing the Chinese phrase “Gung Ho” to the Marine Corps. Carlson’s Raiders first saw combat in the raid on Makin Island on 17th August 1942. Edson’s Raiders fought at Tulagi at the start of the Solomon Islands campaign and then were transferred to help defend Henderson Field on Guadalcanal. It was here that Edson earned the Medal of Honor for his defence of Lunga Ridge (later known as Edson’s Ridge) against 2,500 Japanese troops on the night of 13th-14th September. The Raiders were eventually disbanded in February 1944 and reintegrated back into the Marine Corps where their members continued to excel – three of the six men in the iconic flag-raising on Iwo Jima were ex-Raiders.Cost60pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LComposition1 NCO and 3 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 6 men with rifles for +15pts each- The NCO and up to 3 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 3 men may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts each.- Up to 3 men may replace their rifle with a shotgun for +2pts each- The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Stubborn- Tough Fighters- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken) US Marines move cautiously through the jungleBOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   40BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   4006/12/2024   12:21

41THE ARMY LISTMERRILL’S MARAUDERS SQUAD The Americans were impressed by the raids conducted by Orde Wingate’s Chindits in 1943 and agreed to create their own long-range penetration jungle warfare under the code name Galahad. The 5307th Composite Unit (Provisional) was an all-volunteer force drawn from experienced jungle fighters in the US Army including veterans of Guadalcanal and New Guinea. After training in India, the force of nearly 3,000 men was committed to fighting in the north alongside the Chinese X and Y Forces under the overall command of General Stillwell. Journalists soon dubbed the unit “Merrill’s Marauders” after their leader Brigadier General Frank Merrill. The marauders performed exceptionally well, exacting a heavy toll on the Japanese in ambushes and hit-and run raiding. However, just as with the Chindits, they suffered grievous losses from wounds and exhaustion. They finally had to be withdrawn after the battle of Myitkyina left them with 130 combat-effective officers and men out of an original 2,997. Only two of the men who had entered Burma had survived without being hospitalised at some point due to wounds or disease.Cost85pts (Veteran)PeriodLComposition1 NCO and 4 menWeaponsRiflesOptions- Add up to 7 men with rifles at +17 pts each  - The NCO and up to 3 men may replace their rifle with a submachine gun for +4pts each- Up to 2 men may replace their rifle with an automatic rifle for +6pts each.- The squad can be given anti-tank grenades for +2pts per figureSpecial Rules- Stubborn- Tough Fighters- Fieldcraft- Long-range Penetration: When outflanking, as described in the Bolt Action rulebook (see page 172), Merrill’s Marauders may re-roll failed order tests for coming onto the table.- Tank Hunters (if anti-tank grenades taken).PHILIPPINE SCOUTS SQUAD  The Philippine Scouts were first established in 1901 to assist against the Philippine revolution and later helped to subdue the Moro tribes on the island of Mindanao. The Scouts were well disciplined and fought hard, generally under American officers. Starting in 1910 the US Army sent one promising candidate per year to West Point. In July 1941 there were 15 Filipino officers in the division. The primary US man-portable anti-tank weapon of the Second World War was the M1 series of rocket launchers, better known as ‘Bazooka’ – a name stemming from the comedy musical instrument of the same name which bore a distinct resemblance to the simple, tubular weapon. BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST54VEHICLESThe entry of each vehicle includes, for your convenience, information about the period of the war during which the vehicle saw most of its service. It also includes the number of units produced during the war, which of course, is normally an approximation.TANKS Armoured Divisions were the most powerful battle-winning formations of the war, and tanks were the most important elements within them. American manufacturing strategy was to optimise production by limiting the number of basic designs as much as possible. The same hull was often adapted to serve different functions; the chassis of the M4 medium tank was used to make the M10 and M36 tank destroyers as well as Gun Motor Carriages (M12 or M30, with 155mm guns). The tank itself could also carry a howitzer. This extreme versatility was fundamental for both maintenance and resupply. It was also much easier to recover and repair vehicles that shared commonality of parts. Field recovery and repair units proved very adept at dealing with battle damage and putting tanks back into the field.M3 STUART LIGHT TANK Before the war much effort went into building light tanks, which were felt to have a role analogous to cavalry in an earlier age. Once war began these precepts were quickly brought into question, and the need for more heavily armoured and better-armed tanks became apparent. The M3 was derived from the pre-war M2A4 light tank: it retained that tank’s 37mm gun and two hull-mounted machine guns. During the war the M3 underwent many improvements both in terms of its armour and its weaponry. The early version described here had 38mm of armour on the hull and turret front and as much as 44mm on the lower hull casting. The sides and rear had 25mm of armour. Improved versions had a slightly thicker turret mantlet at 51mm, but other armour remained the same. With a maximum speed of 36mph and range of around 70 miles, it could easily perform in a reconnaissance role. The chief flaw in the design was the fact that the rivets sometimes ‘popped’ in the event of a hit. Principal service: 1940–42. In addition to light tanks a new specification was laid down for a medium tank, with the objective of delivering 2,000 tanks over eighteen months. The M3 medium tank was a stopgap response that shared the same Wright radial air-cooled gasoline engine and vertical volute suspension of the pre-war M2 medium tank. The maximum armour thickness was 56mm and the turret and sponson were cast. The rest of the hull was of riveted construction. The tank featured a 360° rotating turret with a cupola-mounted machine gun, and a 75mm gun (with limited traverse) mounted in a sponson on the right-hand side. Gyro-stabilisers that operated in the vertical plain were fitted to both guns. Two versions were manufactured from the start: the standard M3 and the M3 modified to meet British specifications. The latter featured, amongst many detail improvements, a lower profile turret incorporating the radio (deleting the turret cupola mounted machine gun). In British service the original US spec M3 with cupola was known as the General Lee, whilst the M3 with lower profile turret was known as the General Grant. 77THE ARMY LISTARMOURED CARSThe US forces made little use of armoured cars in the reconnaissance role, preferring to employ light tanks instead. As a result the Chiefs of Staff didn’t see the need for a variety of armoured cars, and the only vehicle adopted by the American army was the M8 Greyhound. In fact a heavy armoured car was developed – the T17 series – but was not used by frontline American forces although it was used by the British and was exported after the war.M8 GREYHOUND The M8 was developed following evaluation of the armoured vehicles in service with other nations. Ford-built armoured cars started rolling off the production lines in March 1943. These vehicles were highly manoeuvrable thanks to six-wheel drive, a weight of just 17,468 lbs, and a low silhouette that made concealment easy. The 37mm gun, paired with a coaxial 7.62mm machine gun, was often supplemented by a ring-mounted HMG. The vehicle could reach a maximum on-road speed of 55mph. Principal service: 1943–45. BOLT ACTIONTHE ARMY LIST80TRANSPORTS AND TOWSM2 and M3 half-tracks provided ideal towing vehicles as well as transports for the front-line fighting units. The M3 was large enough to move all but the largest guns together with crew and ammunition. Originally intended for the Armoured Divisions, export versions of these vehicles were also used by the British and Russians. When it came to trucks, US industry proved capable of producing abundant transportation for the US and Allied armies. Trucks and tractors were manufactured in a variety of sizes from the tiny Jeeps to huge recovery tractors and heavy artillery tows. These are usually identified in terms of their weight, for example the ‘Dodge ¾ ton’. The iconic Jeep became one of the most obvious success stories of the war.M3 WHITE SCOUT CAR  The M3 was too large and heavy to be considered a proper scout car, but its transport capacity (eight men and a driver) made it suitable for a number of tasks including that of armoured transport and reconnaissance vehicle. The same basic design was used to develop the M3 half-track. Although the White Scout Car carried an HMG it was often fitted with a further two MMGs along the sides. The M3 Scout Car was used by the Americans, British and Russians. Principal service: 1940–44. 85THE ARMY LISTDUKW AMPHIBIOUS TRUCK  In 1942 the army needed an amphibious truck that could land men on the beach and then shuttle supplies from ships to shore. Beginning with a basic 2½ ton truck, a fully amphibious version was developed known as the DUKW – a series of letters that simply designates its model year (1942), type and drive format – unsurprisingly it was referred to by the troops as a ‘Duck’. The six-wheel drive DUKW could also function as a regular truck and tow light and medium artillery pieces. 21,000 were built.Cost22pts (Inexperienced), 27pts (Regular), 32pts (Veteran)PeriodM/LWeaponsNoneDamage Value6+ (soft-skin)Transport12 menTowLight or medium anti-tank gun; light or medium howitzer; light anti-aircraft gunOptions- May have a pintle-mounted MMG with a 360-degree arc of fire for +20pts- May upgrade the MMG to an HMG for +10ptsSpecial Rules- AmphibiousLVT AMPHIBIOUS TROOP TRANSPORT  The LVT (Landing Vehicle Tracked) series was developed to provide a fully amphibious troop transport for beach assaults in the Pacific Theatre. These vehicles were armed with machine guns and could transport two fully equipped squads of Marines. The name “Water Buffalo” was used to describe them – in British usage this became simply “Buffalo”. Principal service: 1943–44. 91SAMPLE ARMIESThere are several different ways to build an army, all depending on how you and your friends want to play Bolt Action. Do you want to faithfully re-create a specific unit? Do you want to create an army in opposition to your friends’, or do you want to build a ‘competitive’ force to take to tournaments? All these options are equally valid!Creating a force that represents a specific unit or formation at a particular time during the war can be very rewarding. Many people choose to recreate the unit one of their relatives fought in, or a unit representative of their home nation, while others may be fascinated by a particular battle or theatre and want to create an army around that. Whatever your reason for being drawn to a specific army, creating a force that accurately represents it can be deeply satisfying. It does require a little bit of research to know how particular forces were organised during the Second World War, what equipment they had, and what markings they displayed, but such ‘deep dive’ learning about the war and how it was fought can be enormously rewarding in its own right! If you have a group of friends with whom you play regularly, it can be an idea to discuss which armies you would each like to play. Whilst not strictly necessary, it certainly adds to the verisimilitude of the experience if historically accurate foes face off against each other on the tabletop! One way this can be achieved is by picking a theatre, such as the Pacific or North-West Europe, and splitting the forces along Axis and Allied lines. Another way would be to build two forces, one Allied and one Axis - this way you can still play a game against your friend who currently only has an Allied army, for example. Knowing what forces your regular opponents will be playing can be a good way to inform the force you choose.Finally, tournaments offer a different challenge. There are many different types of tournaments, with different force selection requirements; some require armies from specific theatres or periods while others allow free rein in army choice. In each case, the tournament organiser will inform you of such requirements. Tournaments, by their nature, are more competitive environments, generally with an emphasis on making the ‘best’ army over a strictly historically accurate one. However, an army built in isolation, such as for a tournament, can be ideal to take to a club to play against other players with whom you are unfamiliar. Such ‘pick up’ games, as they are sometimes known, are a great way to make new friends and play against new armies.What follows are example American armies built using the Force Selection rules, showing the wide versatility available in Armies of the United States.  American troops bring their firepower to bear as their German foes are forced through a bottleneckBOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   91BOLT Action US Armies Layouts.indd   9106/12/2024   12:24

101FORCE SELECTION GUIDES In Bolt Action, you can simply select your force by mixing up squads and vehicles from any one provisional force list or one of the Armies of series of books, based on your collection of models. Such force could sometimes be a mix of infantry, artillery and vehicles that technically never saw action together in reality, because they were used in the war at different times. This is fine, but some players prefer to field forces that are more historically accurate. In this book, which includes American units from the entire span of the war, we have decided to provide you with three helpful Selection Guides, to give you some guidance as to which units should and should not fielded together to maintain a higher degree of historical accuracy. These Guides are for Early War, Mid-War and Late War, which we arbitrarily define as follows:  Early War*Mid-WarLate War1939–1941*1942–19431944–1945Keep in mind that these guides are just guidelines, and we have made some generalisations that surely would have seen plenty of exceptions during the war. For example, we have not included M6 Gun Motor Carriages in the Late War Guide, as they were mostly out of use by then, but it’s very likely that the odd one did turn up in some engagement where old-fashioned and out of date equipment was used in a pinch. The chronological opposite could also be true, for example troops might have field-tested prototypes of weapons still in development, like some of the ground-based rocket launcher systems, before they were officially entered into service. * EARLY WARAlthough the US did not officially enter the war until the final days of the 'Early' period, we have intentionally labelled some units as 'Early'. This provides a guide to what a force representing the Americans at the very start of their involvement might look like, and also allows players to create 'what-if' forces! Battery LL sinks the Hayate by Peter Dennis © Osprey Publishing Ltd.
